title: Colombian Military Transport Plane Crashes After Takeoff, Killing 66
description: At least 66 people died and dozens were injured when a Colombian Air Force C-130 Hercules transport plane crashed shortly after taking off near the border with Peru.
lede: A Colombian Air Force plane carrying troops crashed shortly after takeoff in the south of the country, leaving at least 66 people dead and dozens injured. The disaster, described by defense officials as a tragic accident, is one of the deadliest in the recent history of Colombia's military.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: A US-made Lockheed Martin C-130 Hercules transport plane crashed near the town of Puerto Leguízamo in Putumayo province. The aircraft had just taken off when it came down close to the border with Peru. According to Air Force commander Carlos Fernando Silva Rueda, the plane was carrying 114 army personnel and 11 crew members at the time of the incident.

Colombian Defence Minister Pedro Sánchez described the event as a "tragic accident" while the plane was transporting security forces. Following the crash, ammunition on board the aircraft detonated as a result of a subsequent fire, further complicating the disaster and rescue efforts.

Emergency workers were immediately dispatched to the site, searching through the wreckage for survivors. Images from local media showed a large plume of smoke rising from the crash area, with locals reportedly transporting injured soldiers to nearby hospitals on the backs of small motorbikes.

sections.heading: The Investigation
paragraphs: Authorities have launched an investigation into the exact cause of the crash. While Defence Minister Sánchez confirmed it was an accident, further details on what led the Hercules C-130 to come down have not yet been established. The detonation of onboard ammunition due to the fire has severely damaged the wreckage, complicating forensic analysis.

The death toll has been reported as at least 66 by multiple sources, with a military source telling AFP that the casualties included 58 soldiers, six air force personnel, and two police officers. Reuters also corroborated the figure of 66 dead, emphasizing the severe loss of life in this military transport operation.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: This crash marks one of the deadliest aviation accidents in the recent history of the Colombian Air Force. It highlights the inherent risks faced by military personnel operating in remote areas and relying on aging transport infrastructure. The loss of such a significant number of troops in a single event is a profound blow to the nation's security forces.

The incident has also brought immediate political scrutiny to the state of Colombia's military equipment. President Gustavo Petro took to social media to express his grief, stating that the "horrendous accident ... should not have happened." His comments quickly turned to the broader issues of military readiness and bureaucratic hurdles.

sections.heading: The Bigger Picture
paragraphs: President Petro used the tragedy to highlight ongoing delays in modernizing the armed forces' equipment. In a lengthy post on X, he blamed "bureaucratic problems" for stalling necessary upgrades to military aircraft. "I will allow no further delays, the lives of our young people are at stake," he declared, pointing to systemic issues beyond this single crash.

This disaster occurs within a broader context of military aviation safety in South America. Just last month, a similar incident occurred when a Bolivian Air Force C-130 Hercules, which was transporting banknotes, crashed in the western part of that country, resulting in the deaths of at least 20 people.

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: In the immediate aftermath, the focus remains on the recovery operations and providing medical care to the dozens of injured personnel. The military will need to secure the crash site and ensure all remains are recovered and families are notified, while managing the logistical challenges in the remote Putumayo province.

Politically, President Petro's statements suggest an impending push to fast-track military modernization programs. The tragedy is likely to be used as a catalyst to overcome the bureaucratic hurdles he mentioned, potentially leading to immediate reviews of current transport aircraft fleets and expedited procurement of new equipment.
